1:
Ps 83: Asaph's Pan-National Enemy Lament — Ten Nations Conspiring Against Israel. The final Asaph psalm (Pss 50, 73-83 form the Asaph collection). Ps 83 contains the longest enemy-list in the Psalter: ten nations or peoples conspiring to destroy Israel. The historical occasion is uncertain — some have connected it to Jehoshaphat's crisis (2 Chr 20: Moab, Ammon, and Meunites attacking), or to the post-exilic period, or to an eschatological scenario. The sheer breadth of the coalition (including Assyria, v8) suggests either a telescoping of multiple threats or a comprehensive future vision.

'O God, do not keep silence; do not hold your peace or be still, O God!' — the urgent summons for divine speech: three synonyms of divine silence (al-tachresh al-tishqot al-tishhot) as the thing to be avoided. Divine silence = divine inaction. The enemies are active and vocal (vv2-8); the problem is YHWH's apparent silence in contrast.
2: 'For behold, your enemies make an uproar; those who hate you have raised their heads' — the enemies are both YHWH's enemies (oyevecha) and Israel's: the equation is a covenant claim. 'Raised their heads' (naseu rosh) — the posture of self-assertion and aggression: the lifted head is the opposite of the bowed head of submission (cf. Ps 3:3: YHWH who lifts the psalmist's head against those who have raised their own).
3: 'They lay crafty plans against your people; they consult together against your treasured ones' — 'crafty plans' (ya'arimu sod, they scheme with cunning) — political conspiracy, the war council. 'Your treasured ones' (tzephunechah) — those whom YHWH has hidden/treasured (tzaphan, to hide safely). The enemies target precisely what YHWH has chosen to protect.
4: 'They say, Come, let us wipe them out as a nation; let the name of Israel be remembered no more!' — the explicit goal of the coalition: national annihilation (venachridem migoy, we will cut them off from being a nation) and name-erasure (velo yizakher shem Yisrael od, let the name of Israel be remembered no more). This is genocide: not merely defeat but the elimination of corporate memory. The nations want what Pharaoh attempted (Exod 1:8-22) — the elimination of the covenant people as a people.
5: 'For they conspire with one accord; against you they make a covenant' — the nations form a counter-covenant (berith) against YHWH and Israel. The anti-covenant coalition: using the covenant-language to organize opposition to the covenant people. In ANE diplomacy, defensive and offensive alliances were ratified by treaty-covenant; the nations have formed such a treaty against YHWH's covenant people.
6: 'The tents of Edom and the Ishmaelites, Moab and the Hagrites' — the enemy list begins. Edom (southern neighbor, Esau's descendants), Ishmaelites (descendants of Ishmael, son of Abraham and Hagar; northern Arabia), Moab (eastern neighbor, Lot's descendants), Hagrites (an Arab tribe related to Hagar).
7: 'Gebal and Ammon and Amalek, Philistia with the inhabitants of Tyre' — continued: Gebal (Phoenician city/Byblos, or the Edomite Gebal), Ammon (eastern neighbor, Lot's descendants), Amalek (the perpetual enemy from Exod 17; Num 24:20), Philistia (the coastal enemy of the judges and early monarchy period), Tyre (the major Phoenician trading city, sometimes ally but often hostile).
8: 'Assyria also has joined them; they are the strong arm of the children of Lot' — Assyria (Ashur) as the military superpower backing the coalition: the 'strong arm' (zeroa = arm/strength) of the smaller nations. 'Children of Lot' — both Moab and Ammon were Lot's descendants (Gen 19:37-38). Assyrian backing of smaller states against Israel/Judah is historically attested in the 8th century (Tig-lath-pileser III's campaigns). The inclusion of Assyria gives the coalition both local and imperial dimensions.
9: 'Do to them as you did to Midian, as to Sisera and Jabin at the river Kishon' — the historical precedents invoked: (1) Midian — Gideon's victory over the Midianite coalition in Judg 7-8; (2) Sisera — Deborah and Barak's victory over Sisera (Judg 4-5); (3) Jabin — the Canaanite king who commanded Sisera. These are the Judges-period military victories where YHWH routed vastly superior forces through unexpected means. The prayer asks for the pattern to repeat.
10: '...who were destroyed at En-dor, who became dung for the ground' — En-dor (near the Valley of Jezreel) was likely the site of one of the battles of Judg 4-5; Sisera's army was destroyed in this region. 'Became dung for the ground' (hayu domen la-adamah) — the most extreme form of ANE disgrace: unburied bodies decomposing into fertilizer. No burial = no honor, no rest, no ancestral memory. The enemies of YHWH become manure.
11: 'Make their nobles like Oreb and Zeeb, all their princes like Zebah and Zalmunna' — the Midianite leaders killed by Gideon: Oreb ('raven') and Zeeb ('wolf') were killed in Judg 7:25; Zebah ('sacrifice') and Zalmunna ('withdrawn shade/protection') were captured and killed by Gideon personally in Judg 8:5-21. These names were preserved as victory-trophies in Israel's memory. The prayer invokes specific named enemies as precedents for the current enemy leaders.
12: '...who said, Let us take possession for ourselves of the pastures of God' — the enemies' goal stated in their own words: to seize ne'ot Elohim (the pastures/habitations of God) — the land of Canaan that YHWH claims as his own estate. The land is YHWH's pasture (Lev 25:23: 'the land is mine; you are strangers and sojourners with me'); the enemies want to seize it. The economic motivation of empire — taking the best agricultural land — is expressed in theological terms.
13: 'O my God, make them like whirling dust, like chaff before the wind' — two dispersion images: galgal (tumbleweed/whirling wheel) — the dry thistle or wheel of chaff spinning across the open field before the wind. 'Chaff before the wind' (qash lifnei ruach) — the harvesting metaphor: threshed grain has the chaff blown away. Both images express complete scattering: the coalition's unity dissolved and each member blown in a different direction.
14: 'As fire consumes the forest, as the flame sets the mountains ablaze' — the fire-imagery of total destruction: forest fire as YHWH's weapon. ANE rulers used fire as an instrument of conquest (burning cities, forests, crops); Ps 83 invokes divine fire against the enemy coalition. The mountain fire corresponds to the theophanic fire of Sinai and the eschatological fire of judgment.
15: 'So may you pursue them with your tempest and terrify them with your hurricane!' — the storm-theophany as weapon: suphah (tempest/whirlwind) and searah (stormy wind). These are the same storm-forces that YHWH directed at the Egyptians at the sea (Exod 14:21) and that Ps 29 attributes to the divine storm-lord. The prayer: let YHWH deploy his cosmic storm against the national coalition.
16: 'Fill their faces with shame, that they may seek your name, O LORD' — the purpose of judgment: shame (boshet) leading to seeking (yevaqshu shimecha, they may seek your name). The divine judgment is not merely punitive but pedagogical: the enemies' shame is intended to drive them toward seeking YHWH. This anticipates the universal conversion vision of Isa 19:22 (Egypt struck and then healed) and the NT mission to the Gentiles.
17: 'Let them be put to shame and dismayed forever; let them perish in disgrace' — the harder prayer: if shame does not produce seeking (v16), then permanent disgrace. The two-track petition (v16 seeks conversion; v17 seeks judgment) reflects the OT covenant pattern: blessing for the obedient, curse for the persistent rebel. The 'forever' (leolam) of v17 is the eschatological permanence of irreversible judgment.
18: 'That they may know that you alone, whose name is the LORD, are the Most High over all the earth' — the final theological statement of the psalm: the goal of all the judgment is knowledge. Yeda'u ki attah levadechah shemechah YHWH elyon al-kol haaretz (that they may know that you alone, your name is YHWH, are Most High over all the earth). The nations should come to know (the Exodus-knowledge formula: 'that they may know I am the LORD') through the judgment what they refused to know through revelation. This is the most universalist verse of the Asaph collection.
